results.possibleIdentity: Iris Crump (often referred to in historical documents as an African American artist and activist active in the late 1960s and 1970s) is likely the individual intended by the name Iris Krump. She was a prominent figure in the New York City art activism scene, notably as a member of the Art Workers Coalition (AWC). In 1969, she participated in the AWC's 'Open Hearing' at the School of Visual Arts and was a co-founder of the AWC's 'Black Blok' committee alongside Faith Ringgold and Tom Lloyd, which advocated for increased representation of Black artists in major museums like the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA). Crump was also an early member of 'Where We At' (WWA), a pioneering collective of Black women artists founded in 1971. Her artistic practice included sculpture and mixed-media works; critics noted that she incorporated lights and mirrors into contemporary settings to depict communal activities. She was part of the 'X 12' exhibition of women artists at the Museum gallery in Noho. In 1982, she received a National Endowment for the Arts (NEA) Visual Artists' Fellowship in sculpture. Historical records from rare book catalogs list her name in proximity to the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), and her use of technological elements like controlled lighting in her installations aligns with the organization's cross-disciplinary goals.

results.possibleIdentity: Peter Deal was a prominent British fashion and beauty photographer active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He trained in New York under legendary photographers Irving Penn and Bert Stern, and he reportedly worked as an assistant on Marilyn Monroe's famous 'The Last Sitting' session. Deal was known for his meticulous black-and-white printing and high-fashion portraiture. His work appeared on the covers of major magazines, including Harper's Bazaar UK (notably the April 1968 issue). In 1970, he was associated with an exhibition at the Institute of Contemporary Arts (ICA) in London. He is also remembered as a mentor to other photographers, such as Jim Rice, during the late 1960s and early 1970s.

Paul H. Deal was a microbiologist and scientist at NASA Ames Research Center who was active in research during the late 1960s and 1970s. He frequently co-authored technical papers with prominent NASA researchers such as Richard S. Young, including the 1970 study 'Altered gravitational field effects on the fertilized frog egg.' Given the mission of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) to foster collaborations between artists, engineers, and scientists, Paul Deal represents a likely technical collaborator from the scientific community. In the late 1980s, he transitioned into software development, creating evolution-based computer programs mentioned in Scientific American and the text-based role-playing game 'Fallthru' (1988).
